worth_it_explanation: Asset ownership decides who keeps control of the ad account, the Page, the pixel or dataset, and the catalog when a person leaves or a partnership ends. When those assets sit in a personal profile or an agency's Business Manager rather than the brand's own, the brand can lose access to its advertising history, audiences, and conversion signal with no clean way to recover them. The check is mostly reading. Business Settings lists every asset and the business that owns it, so an auditor can map the gaps in an afternoon. The fix is a claim or transfer request plus a permissions cleanup, not a rebuild, and it protects the account against a single departure or a soured vendor relationship.
intro: Asset ownership in Meta Business Suite covers which business holds the ad account, the Page, the pixel or dataset, and the product catalog. Meta treats the owning business as the one with final control, so an asset owned by a personal profile or by a partner's Business Manager rather than the brand's own can be lost when that person leaves or the partnership ends. These checks confirm that the brand's data-capturing assets live in a business the brand controls, that partner access is granted through sharing rather than ownership, and that the owning business has the protections to survive a single admin leaving.

audit_checks.title: Core advertising assets are owned by a personal profile instead of the brand's Business Manager
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An ad account, Page, or pixel tied to a personal profile rather than a business follows the person, not the brand. When that person leaves or loses access to their Facebook account, the brand can be locked out of its advertising history, custom audiences, and conversion data with no administrative path to recover them.

educate.base:
Meta lets a person run ads and manage a Page from a personal Facebook profile without ever creating a Business Manager. The ad account, the Page, and the pixel then belong to that individual rather than to a business the brand controls. Meta Business Suite is the place where a brand brings those assets under a business it owns, through Business Settings, so the brand holds final administrative control rather than depending on one person's login.

When core assets stay on a personal profile, the brand's access to its own advertising rests on that individual remaining reachable and cooperative. If the person leaves the company, deactivates their account, or simply stops responding, the brand can lose the ad account's spending history, the custom audiences built inside it, and the conversion signal the pixel or dataset has accumulated. Rebuilding audiences and conversion history from scratch sets the account's measurement and optimization back to a cold start.

Bringing an asset into a Business Manager is a claim, not a copy. The owning business gains administrative control while the history and audiences stay intact. A brand that pays the invoices and captures the data is, in Meta's own guidance, the party that should own those assets.

A few signs point to assets sitting on a personal profile rather than a business:
  • The account has no Business Manager, and ads run from a personal profile's Ads Manager
  • A Business Manager exists, but the Page or pixel appears under a person's access rather than as a business-owned asset
  • Only one individual can grant access to the ad account, and there is no business that can override them
You may want to confirm whether each core asset is owned by a business the brand controls before treating the setup as stable. Ownership and day-to-day access are different things, and only ownership survives a departure.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the pixel or dataset and the product catalog carry years of purchase events and audiences that drive Advantage+ shopping campaigns and dynamic ads. If those assets sit on a personal profile, a single departure can cut the store off from its conversion history and its catalog at once. You may want to confirm the catalog and the dataset are both owned by a business the brand controls, not only shared into it.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the pixel or dataset holds the lead events and the audiences built from them, and the Page hosts the lead forms. When these live on a personal profile, losing that person can mean losing the lead history that trains bidding and the forms that collect new leads. You may want to confirm the Page and the dataset are owned by the brand's business rather than an individual.

learn_more.source_title: Add People to a Business Portfolio and Assign a Business Asset
Assign Business Assets to People in Your Business Portfolio

investigate.intro: Confirm whether a Business Manager exists for the brand and whether each core asset (the ad account, the Page, and the pixel or dataset) is owned by that business rather than by a personal profile.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Meta Business Suite and go to Business Settings for the brand's business.
Under 'Accounts', review 'Ad accounts', 'Pages', and 'Data sources'. Each asset listed here is owned by this business. Note any core asset that is missing from these lists.
For any missing asset, check whether it appears under 'Partners' or only under a person's access. An asset the brand uses but does not own will show up as shared rather than owned, or not at all.

facebook_profile_check.label: Personal Profile Ads Manager
steps: If the brand has no Business Manager, open Ads Manager from the personal profile that runs the ads.
Note whether the ad account, the Page, and the pixel are reachable only from this profile. Assets reachable solely from a personal profile are owned by that individual, not by a business.
Record each core asset and the profile it depends on so the ownership gap is documented per asset.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per core asset (ad account, Page, pixel or dataset, catalog), whether it appears as owned by the brand's Business Manager in Business Settings. An asset listed as business-owned passes. An asset reachable only from a personal profile, or shared into the business without being owned by it, is a fail recorded with the asset name and the profile it currently depends on.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each core asset above, whether it is owned by the brand's Business Manager or tied to a personal profile to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that core advertising assets on {{site_name}} are owned by a personal profile rather than a Business Manager the brand controls. Ownership tied to an individual follows that person, so a departure or a lost account login can cut {{site_name}} off from its ad account history, its custom audiences, and its pixel data with no administrative way to recover them. {{voice_recommend}} creating or confirming a Business Manager for {{site_name}}, then claiming the ad account, the Page, and the pixel or dataset into it so the business holds final control. After the assets are claimed, confirm in Business Settings that each one appears as owned by the business and grant individuals access through the business rather than through their personal profiles.

audit_checks.title: The brand's Page and pixel are owned by a partner's Business Manager rather than the brand's own
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the agency or vendor owns the assets that capture the brand's data, the brand holds access at the partner's discretion. If the relationship ends, the partner can revoke access to the Page, the pixel or dataset, and the audiences built from them, and the brand may have no clean way to take the assets with it.

educate.base:
Meta supports two ways for an agency or vendor to work in a brand's account. The partner can own the asset in its own Business Manager, or the brand can own the asset and share access to the partner through the Partners section in Business Settings. Meta's guidance is that the brand should own any asset through which its data is captured, such as the Page and the pixel or dataset, and grant the partner access rather than the other way around.

When the partner owns the data-capturing assets, the brand's relationship to its own Page, conversion data, and audiences runs through the partner's business. That arrangement works while the relationship is healthy. When it ends, the partner controls whether the brand keeps access. A brand that wants to move to a new agency, or bring work in house, can find the Page, the pixel history, and the custom audiences sitting inside a business it does not control and cannot claim without the partner's cooperation.

Partner access and partner ownership look similar in day-to-day use because both let the agency manage the account. The difference appears at the exit. A shared asset returns fully to the brand when access is revoked. An asset owned by the partner stays with the partner unless they agree to transfer it.

A few signs suggest a partner owns assets that the brand should own:
  • The Page or pixel does not appear under 'Accounts' in the brand's Business Settings, only under 'Partners' or not at all
  • The agency, not the brand, is listed as the owning business for the pixel or dataset that fires on the brand's site
  • Access to audiences or conversion data is described as something the agency 'gives' the brand rather than something the brand owns
You may want to map each data-capturing asset to its owning business before assuming the brand can keep it. The asset the brand depends on most for measurement is the one most costly to lose at a handoff.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the pixel or dataset and the catalog feed Advantage+ shopping campaigns and dynamic product ads, and both accumulate value over months. If the agency owns them, switching agencies can mean leaving behind the conversion history and the audiences that make those campaigns work. You may want to confirm the brand's own Business Manager owns the dataset and the catalog, with the agency added as a partner.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the Page hosts the instant forms and the pixel or dataset records the lead events. A partner that owns these holds the lead history and the form definitions. You may want to confirm the brand owns the Page and the dataset, so a change of vendor does not strand the lead data or the forms that collect it.

learn_more.source_title: About Partners in Meta Business Suite

investigate.intro: Map each data-capturing asset (the Page, the pixel or dataset, and the catalog) to the business that owns it, and confirm whether the partner holds ownership or only shared access.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings for the brand's business and review 'Pages' and 'Data sources' under 'Accounts'. Assets listed here are owned by the brand.
Open the 'Partners' section and note which businesses have access and to which assets. An asset the agency manages but that does not appear under the brand's own 'Accounts' is likely owned elsewhere.
For the pixel or dataset, open it in Events Manager and check the owning business shown in its settings. Record whether it is the brand's business or the partner's.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per data-capturing asset (Page, pixel or dataset, catalog), the business that owns it. An asset owned by the brand's business, with the partner present only as shared access, passes. An asset owned by the partner's business is a fail. The brand cannot retain it without the partner's cooperation, so the fail is recorded with the asset name and the owning business.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the owning business for each data-capturing asset above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that data-capturing assets for {{site_name}}, including the Page and the pixel or dataset, are owned by a partner's Business Manager rather than {{site_name}}'s own. Because the partner owns these assets, {{site_name}} holds access at the partner's discretion, and a change of agency or vendor could leave the Page, the conversion data, and the audiences built from it inside a business {{site_name}} does not control. {{voice_recommend}} moving ownership of the data-capturing assets into {{site_name}}'s Business Manager and re-adding the partner through the Partners section with shared access instead of ownership. After the change, confirm in Business Settings that {{site_name}} is the owning business for the Page and the dataset, with the partner listed only as a partner.

audit_checks.title: The owning Business Manager has a single admin and is not business-verified
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Even when the brand owns its assets, a Business Manager with one admin and no business verification concentrates control in a single login. If that admin loses access, no one else can administer the business or recover the assets, and an unverified business has fewer of Meta's recovery and security protections to fall back on.

educate.base:
Owning the assets is the first step. The business that owns them also has to be set up to survive the loss of any one person. A Business Manager with a single admin places every administrative power in one login. If that person leaves, loses access to their Facebook account, or is locked out, no one else can add users, claim assets, or change settings, and the assets the business owns become difficult to administer.

Business verification is the process where Meta confirms a business portfolio belongs to a real organization, using official documents. A verified business has access to recovery paths and security features that an unverified one does not, and verification is required for some advertising and developer options. An owning business that is unverified is both harder to recover if access is lost and limited in what it can do.

The Security Center in Business Settings shows the business's protection status, including whether two-factor authentication is required for the people who can access it. Requiring two-factor on the admins, keeping more than one admin, and completing business verification together reduce the chance that a single compromised or departed login takes the whole business with it.

A few signs point to a fragile owning business:
  • Business Settings lists only one person with the admin role
  • The business shows as not verified in the Security Center or business info
  • Two-factor authentication is not required for the people who can access the business
You may want to treat the owning business itself as an asset to protect, separate from the ad account and the Page. Ownership of the assets matters little if the single key to the business is lost.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the owning business controls the catalog, the dataset, and the campaigns that drive revenue. A single admin with no backup means a locked-out login can freeze the ability to manage live shopping campaigns during a peak sales period. You may want to confirm the store's business has more than one admin and required two-factor before a busy season.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the owning business holds the forms, the lead data, and the audiences. If one admin is the only key and that person leaves, the brand may struggle to administer the lead pipeline or grant a new team member access. You may want to confirm a second admin exists and that business verification is complete.

learn_more.source_title: About Business Verification in Meta Business Suite

investigate.intro: Confirm how many people hold the admin role on the owning business, whether the business is verified, and whether two-factor authentication is required for access.

meta_business_settings.label: Meta Business Settings
steps: Open Business Settings for the owning business and go to 'People'. Count how many people have the admin role. Note whether only one person can administer the business.
Open the Security Center and read the business's verification status and whether two-factor authentication is required for the people with access.
Record the admin count, the verification status, and the two-factor requirement so the resilience of the owning business is documented.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for the owning business, the number of people with the admin role, the business verification status, and whether two-factor authentication is required. A business with more than one admin, verification complete, and two-factor required passes. A business with a single admin, with no verification, or without required two-factor is a fail recorded with the specific gaps found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the admin count, verification status, and two-factor requirement for the owning business above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Business Manager owning {{site_name}}'s advertising assets has a single admin and is not business-verified. Concentrating every administrative power in one login means a departure or a locked-out account could leave no one able to administer the business or recover its assets, and an unverified business has fewer of Meta's recovery and security protections to fall back on. {{voice_recommend}} adding at least one more admin to the owning business, completing business verification, and requiring two-factor authentication for everyone with access through the Security Center. After the change, confirm in Business Settings that more than one admin exists and that the verification and two-factor protections are in place.
